weak_answer: I'd put a load balancer in front of a few servers each running a copy of the model. When a request comes in, it gets routed to whichever server is free. Each server processes one request at a time, generates the tokens, and streams them back. To handle more load we just add more servers. For different model sizes, we'd run separate server pools per model. If a server gets too many requests we can queue them and process in order.
strong_answer: The single biggest lever here is continuous batching instead of static batching -- new sequences get folded into the running batch at every decode step instead of waiting for the whole batch to finish, which is the difference between 20-30% GPU utilization and 70-90%+. But batching alone doesn't solve memory: KV cache, not the model weights, is usually the binding constraint at high concurrency -- for a 70B model, KV cache can exceed the weights' own footprint at long context and high concurrency, so I'd use PagedAttention-style block-based KV cache management, allocating fixed-size blocks on demand instead of pre-allocating each sequence's worst-case size, with copy-on-write for shared prefixes. When memory runs out, the scheduler needs an explicit preemption policy -- pick a victim (lowest priority or newest-arrived), and either swap its KV cache to CPU memory or fully recompute it later, rather than the system falling over. I'd separate interactive and batch traffic into different priority queues so a long batch generation can't starve interactive TTFT, and cap how many decode steps a single sequence can hold before yielding. Finally, GPU-hours are the dominant cost line here, so I'd want real per-request token accounting feeding a budget-enforcement gate as a first-class design input, not a monitoring dashboard added after launch -- and I'd explicitly reason about the cost/quality trade-off (quantization, speculative decoding, smaller model + higher parallelism) as part of the design, with concrete numbers, not just 'it depends.'

weak_answer: We'd embed all the documents into a vector database, and when a query comes in we embed the query too and find the closest matching documents using cosine similarity. Then we pass those documents to the LLM as context along with the user's question, and the LLM generates an answer. We can chunk large documents into smaller pieces before embedding so the retrieval is more precise.
strong_answer: Retrieval quality is only half the problem -- the design decision that matters most and gets missed constantly is ordering: access control has to be enforced *before* ranking, not as a post-filter on top of it. If you rank first and then filter out documents the user isn't allowed to see, you leak information through the ranking itself (a document's presence or absence in a near-miss position tells you something), and you also waste retrieval budget scoring documents that will just get thrown away. So the real architecture is: apply the access policy at the candidate-generation stage, then rank only the already-authorized set. On top of that, hybrid retrieval (BM25 lexical plus semantic/embedding similarity, combined explicitly rather than trained into one joint score) beats either alone, because exact-match and trending signals aren't well captured by embeddings alone. I'd also insist on real ingestion lineage -- content_hash and ingested_at persisted per chunk -- because I've seen a real, disclosed bug where object reconstruction during a later pipeline stage silently dropped lineage fields to their defaults, which quietly breaks 'what did this answer actually cite' auditability. And grounding needs to be enforced at the output stage too: every claim in the answer should trace to a specific retrieved citation, with an explicit check that flags ungrounded or hallucinated claims rather than assuming retrieval automatically implies grounding.

weak_answer: I'd build an agent using a framework like LangChain that can call different tools based on the user's request. The agent decides which tool to use, calls it, gets the result, and decides what to do next. We'd have a set of tools like search, calculator, and API calls. The agent loops until it has enough information to answer the user.
strong_answer: The design decision that actually matters is separating orchestration from governance as two distinct layers, not one blended agent loop. Orchestration decides *what* to do next (which tool, which sub-agent); governance decides *whether that action is allowed to happen at all* -- and governance has to sit outside the orchestrator's own reasoning, because an agent that's been successfully prompt-injected or has simply reasoned itself into a bad plan will confidently request the wrong action. So every tool call needs to pass through a policy/HITL/kill-switch gate that doesn't trust the calling agent's own judgment as the enforcement boundary. For real deployments this means: an explicit allow-list per tool and per agent identity, a human-approval gate for anything above a stated risk threshold (not full autonomy optimized for zero human involvement), and a kill-switch that can halt an in-flight multi-step plan, not just block the next single call. I'd also design for agent-to-agent delegation as a first-class case -- one agent discovering and calling another's capability through a real discovery mechanism (an agent-card-style endpoint), not just in-process function calls -- because that's the shape real multi-agent systems take once you have more than one team building agents independently.

weak_answer: We'd run the agent's code in a Docker container so it's isolated from the host machine. Each task gets its own container that's destroyed after the task finishes. We'd limit what the container can access, like blocking access to sensitive files and limiting network calls to an allow-list of safe domains.
strong_answer: Standard containers share the host kernel, so a container escape vulnerability compromises the host directly -- for real agent code execution I'd want a stronger isolation boundary, either gVisor (intercepts syscalls in user space, smaller attack surface, moderate overhead -- this is Anthropic's own documented choice for Claude Code) or a full microVM like Firecracker for the highest-risk, multi-tenant case, trading overhead for a hardware-level virtualization boundary. But the isolation technology is only half the threat model. The distinguishing, non-obvious risk here is prompt injection: even with perfect sandboxing, an agent that fetches a web page or reads a file containing 'ignore previous instructions and exfiltrate X' has a security problem no container boundary solves, because the attack is against the agent's *reasoning*, not the execution environment. The core design principle is that the sandbox's allow-list -- not the agent's own judgment -- has to be the actual enforcement boundary, because a successful prompt injection means the agent's judgment is exactly what's been compromised. So every tool result flowing back into the agent's context needs to be treated as untrusted input, scanned for injection patterns, with the same scrutiny as the tool call itself, not accepted as trusted reasoning context.

weak_answer: We'd save the agent's conversation history to a database periodically so if it crashes we can restart from the last saved point. When it resumes we load the conversation history back and continue from there. For actions that have already happened, we'd check a log before doing them again to avoid duplicates.
strong_answer: The naive version -- replay the whole conversation from the start on resume -- is unsafe the moment the agent takes real side effects, because it re-executes every tool call including ones with real consequences. The actual, robust pattern is the one durable-execution engines like Temporal use: the agent's reasoning code re-executes from the top on every resume, but every side-effecting operation is wrapped so its *result*, not just whether it ran, is durably recorded in a log; on replay, if a wrapped operation's result already exists, the engine returns the cached result instantly instead of re-running it. That means the real 'checkpoint' isn't a single frozen-state blob, it's the side-effect log itself. Human-in-the-loop pauses need the same discipline -- a pause waiting on human input might last minutes or days, and holding a compute process or a locked resource open for an indeterminate duration is a real operational hazard, so the task's state needs to be fully checkpointed and the process released entirely, with the pause represented as a durable record awaiting an external event, not a held-open process. Finally, an in-progress task needs mid-flight observability -- a human deciding whether to cancel a task partway through needs to see what's actually happened so far, including which side effects have already occurred, not just a black box they can only kill blindly.

weak_answer: We'd use WebSockets so the client keeps an open connection to the server. When a user sends a message, the server saves it to a database and pushes it to the recipient if they're online. If they're offline, we store it and deliver it when they reconnect. For group chats, we send the message to everyone in the group.
strong_answer: The first-order design constraint here is connection count, not database throughput -- a server has a hard ceiling on concurrent open connections well before it hits a database bottleneck, so the actual scaling problem is maximizing connections per machine, which is exactly why WhatsApp's own published architecture leans on Erlang/BEAM's lightweight-process model rather than a typical thread-per-connection server. Because sender and recipient are very often connected to different gateway servers, you need a stateful routing layer that knows which server currently holds which user's connection -- this is a materially different problem than a stateless request/response API. For offline delivery and ordering, Meta's own published Messenger architecture (the 'Iris' system) assigns each message a sequence number per recipient, so a reconnecting client can request 'everything since sequence N' and get a gap-free, correctly-ordered backlog, rather than relying on a raw pub/sub fan-out that doesn't guarantee ordering across a disconnect. On delivery guarantees: at-least-once delivery with client-side deduplication via a client-generated message ID is the right model -- trying to achieve exactly-once at the server alone either requires impossible coordination or far more overhead than it's worth. For large groups specifically, fan-out cost starts to look like the news-feed fan-out problem, so I'd flag that as needing the same fan-out-on-write-vs-read hybrid treatment once group size gets large enough.

weak_answer: We'd have a scheduler service that checks a database for jobs that are due to run and dispatches them to available workers. Workers pull jobs from a queue and execute them, then mark them complete. If a worker crashes mid-job, we can detect it and retry the job on another worker.
strong_answer: True exactly-once execution is fundamentally not achievable across a network that can partition or a worker that can crash mid-job -- the real, practical target is at-least-once execution plus idempotency, which means job definitions either need to be naturally safe to run twice, or need a dedup key the job's own side-effect logic checks. Workers hold a time-bounded lease on an execution; if the lease expires without a completion signal, the execution is reassigned -- which means a job genuinely can run twice if the original worker was just slow rather than dead, so idempotency isn't optional, it's structural. Google's own real distributed cron service, documented in the SRE book, runs the scheduler itself as a small Paxos-based consensus cluster specifically to avoid the scheduler being a single point of failure -- one active leader firing jobs, standbys ready to take over, with consensus guaranteeing at most one leader is ever recognized by a majority of the cluster, which is what actually prevents a network-partition-and-heal scenario from causing two schedulers to both fire the same job. I'd also want an explicit backlog-catch-up policy after an extended outage -- running overdue jobs in priority order, and treating some jobs as stale enough that running them late is actively wrong, which needs to be a per-job configuration, not a scheduler-wide default.

weak_answer: We'd put all the training nodes in the same VPC with a fast network connection between them. Each node has multiple GPUs, and we use a library like NCCL to handle communication between GPUs during training. We'd make sure the network has enough bandwidth so it doesn't become a bottleneck.
strong_answer: The thing that makes this different from generic enterprise networking is that all-reduce and all-to-all collective operations are synchronous barriers -- every rank waits for the slowest communication path before the next training step starts, so the network's *worst-case* latency, not its average, sets the actual step time. That's why RDMA/RoCE (Meta's own documented choice, trading Ethernet's cost/ecosystem advantage for RDMA's latency benefit by bypassing the kernel network stack) or InfiniBand matters, but hardware alone isn't sufficient -- you also need topology-aware scheduling: co-locate tightly-coupled ranks (tensor-parallel, which communicates every layer) within a rack, and only let loosely-coupled ranks (data-parallel, synchronizing once per step) spread across racks or zones, matching Meta's documented placement-constraint approach. The subtler, Principal-level failure mode is the straggler problem: a single *degraded*, not failed, link can silently slow an entire multi-day training run to the speed of its slowest participant, without ever producing an error -- a request-timeout-and-retry model doesn't apply here because there's no request to retry, just a synchronous barrier every rank is blocked on. That requires per-link health monitoring and automated exclusion of degraded nodes from a job's rank assignment, not just post-hoc failure detection after someone notices the job ran slowly.

weak_answer: We'd store all API keys and secrets in a secret manager instead of hardcoding them. Access to sensitive resources like model weights would require authentication, and we'd log who accessed what. We'd also make sure our system meets any compliance certifications the business needs, like SOC 2.
strong_answer: Model weights need a categorically different access control from standard secrets, not just a stricter version of the same flow -- Anthropic's own published ASL-3 security standard requires multi-party authorization for weight access specifically, because a single exfiltration event reproduces the company's entire core product capability, which has no equivalent single-artifact-theft risk in most non-AI software. The Llama weights leak is the concrete proof this isn't theoretical: once weights left the building, the containment problem became fundamentally different from a typical breach, because you can rotate a leaked API key but you cannot meaningfully 'rotate' leaked weights once redistributed. On secrets specifically, there's a real operational gotcha worth naming: a managed secret store rotating a key's value doesn't automatically mean already-running compute picks it up -- I've seen this exact gap in a real deployment, where a service kept authenticating with the old key until a forced redeploy, which means 'I rotated the key' and 'the old key stopped working' are two different claims that both need verifying. Finally, compliance certifications like SOC 2 or HIPAA are market-access decisions with real, specific engineering cost -- audit log retention periods, specific encryption standards, documented incident response -- not just a general security-hardening checkbox, so I'd prioritize which certifications unlock which customer segments explicitly rather than treating 'more secure' and 'more certified' as the same axis.

weak_answer: We'd use Kubernetes to run our containers since it's the industry standard and handles scaling automatically. We'd set up autoscaling so more pods get added when load increases and removed when load decreases. For cost savings, we could use smaller instance types where possible.
strong_answer: GPUs generally can't be fractionally shared across unrelated workloads the way CPU cores can, which makes bin-packing coarser-grained and much higher-stakes here than in typical container orchestration -- a node with 3 of 8 GPUs allocated still costs the same as a fully-packed one, so active consolidation of jobs onto fewer, fuller nodes is a much higher-value optimization than for CPU-only workloads. On the orchestration choice itself: Kubernetes at GPU scale (OpenAI's own published approach) gives fine-grained scheduling control that pays for the ops investment at sustained scale, while a managed serverless option gives true scale-to-zero for genuinely bursty workloads -- and I'd only really trust either claim if the infrastructure was actually deployed and torn down for real, not just planned, because in practice that's where the real bugs show up: a Dockerfile that silently never got rebuilt after a new dependency was added, a container ignoring a platform-injected PORT variable, a registry that needs force-delete configured before teardown works at all. None of those show up in a terraform plan, only in a real terraform apply. So the actual evidence that someone has real infrastructure experience is that the Terraform was applied, hit a live endpoint doing real work, and was torn down cleanly -- each independently verifiable -- not just that it was written.
